tagline: Frontier long-horizon agent that rewards terse high-level specs + explicit intent/boundaries — and punishes Opus-era enumerate-every-rule, show-your-work prompting.
effort_line: effort (low|medium|high|xhigh|max) is the only reasoning/verbosity dial — adaptive thinking is ALWAYS ON and cannot be disabled (type:'disabled' rejected, no budget_tokens). Default and recommended START is `high` (NOT xhigh like Opus); lower-effort Fable often exceeds xhigh on prior models, so defaulting xhigh/max wastes cost and causes unrequested refactoring/option-surveying/scope creep.
idioms: Give it the hardest, fully-specified version of the problem and let it scope/ask/execute — 'start at the top of your difficulty range'; it's only marginal on easy tasks.
Steer each behavior with ONE brief high-level instruction (brevity, boundaries, checkpoints) rather than enumerated rule lists — brief now beats exhaustive on this model.
guardrail_extra: NEVER instruct it to echo, transcribe, reflect on, or 'show your thinking'/explain internal reasoning — this trips the reasoning_extraction classifier, returns stop_reason 'refusal', and forces fallback to Opus 4.8.
verify_note: Fresh-context verifier subagents outperform self-critique for checking Fable's work; it dispatches parallel/long-lived subagents more readily than Opus, so orchestrate asynchronously (delegate independent subtasks and keep working).
notes: Released 2026-06-09 as claude-fable-5 (1M context, 128k max output, $10/M in + $50/M out); claude-mythos-5 is the same model without safety classifiers (Project Glasswing). Availability has been in flux (pulled then 'Redeploying Fable 5') — keep an Opus 4.8 fallback wired. New refusal contract: safety classifiers (offensive cyber, bio/life-sciences, reasoning-extraction) return stop_reason:'refusal' as a 200; you aren't billed for a pre-output refusal and fallback credit refunds the prompt-cache retry cost (>95% of sessions need no fallback). For long async runs define a client-side send_to_user tool to surface verbatim content mid-turn (tool inputs are never summarized) — but you must also instruct it to call the tool, or it rarely does. In the final user-facing summary, tell it to drop working shorthand and re-ground someone who saw none of the work. Loop note: Long async runs may end with 'I'll now run X' without the tool call, or ask permission it doesn't need — an autonomous-mode reminder ('continue, do it end to end') fixes both. Provide a markdown memory/notes file (one lesson per file) so it accumulates lessons across runs — a distinctive Fable strength.
